mula: aśocyān anvaśocas tvaṃ prajñā-vādāṃś ca bhāṣase | gatāsūn agatāsūṃś ca nānuśocanti paṇḍitāḥ

doctrinal_renderings.rendering: Kṛṣṇa opens with a double indictment: you mourn those who are not fit objects of mourning (aśocyān), namely Bhīṣma, Droṇa and the rest, who are nitya (eternal) in their paramārtha-svarūpa (ultimate nature), and yet you recite the words of the wise (prajñā-vādān) as though wisdom were already yours. Śaṅkara presses the contradiction hard — you behave like a madman who speaks sense and acts in frenzy at the same moment. The paṇḍitas he invokes are not merely the learned but specifically ātmajñas, those whose paṇḍā (intelligence) rests on ātma-viṣaya (the Self as object); only they do not grieve the dead or the living, because both categories are, in ultimate reality, nitya and therefore aśocya. The teaching that begins here is not consolation — it is diagnosis: Arjuna is mūḍha (deluded), and the rest of the Gītā is the remedy.
doctrinal_renderings.bhashya_anchor: paramārthatastu tān nityān aśocyān anuśocasi ato mūḍho'si — 'in ultimate truth you grieve those who are eternal and ungrievable; therefore you are deluded'

doctrinal_renderings.rendering: Rāmānuja reads 2.11 as a single compound diagnosis: Arjuna suffers from dehātma-svabhava-prajñā — the understanding that the body is the Self — and all his arguments about ancestors losing piṇḍa-offerings and clans being destroyed flow from that one confusion. Kṛṣṇa is not merely calling Arjuna a fool; he is specifying the exact error so that its remedy can be precise. The ātman is neither born nor destroyed (na janmādhīna-sadbhāvo na maraṇādhīna-vināśaś ca), so it furnishes no ground for grief; the body, being acetana (insentient) and pariṇāma-svabhāva (naturally transforming), perishes by its own nature, so it too furnishes no ground for grief. Arjuna's fighting is itself the dharma that constitutes the upāya (means) toward ātma-yāthātmya-avāpti (attainment of the Self's true nature), and refusing it is therefore not piety but ignorance.
doctrinal_renderings.bhashya_anchor: ātmā hi na janmādhīna-sadbhāvo na maraṇādhīna-vināśaś ca — 'the ātman's being does not depend on birth, nor is its destruction dependent on death'

doctrinal_renderings.rendering: Madhva is terse to the point of severity: Arjuna was enveloped in the net of moha (delusion) arising from attachment to relatives, and what he mistakes for wisdom (prajñā-vādān) are only the productions of his own mind (sva-manīṣā-uttha-vacanāni), not the śāstra. The Dvaita gloss holds the distinction absolutely: jīvas are eternally distinct from Hari, their grief and delusion are real states of a real finite being, and the paṇḍitas who do not grieve the dead (gatāsūn) are those who correctly understand Hari's sovereign governance over all life and death. To grieve is to misattribute agency — as though Arjuna, not Hari, were the cause of what will happen on Kurukṣetra.
doctrinal_renderings.bhashya_anchor: prajñā-vādān sva-manīṣā-uttha-vacanāni — 'the so-called words of wisdom are only the productions of his own private mind'

doctrinal_renderings.rendering: Vallabha embeds 2.11 inside a larger architectonic: Kṛṣṇa begins sāṃkhya-buddhi (discriminative understanding) as the first of many upāyas (approaches) — including vairāgya (dispassion), jñāna (knowledge), yoga and prema (love) — each capable alone of bringing siddhi if held with dṛḍhatā (firmness). The double reading of gatāsūn / agatāsūn is explicit: they can mean the dead and the living, or they can mean the inert and the conscious — the body (jaḍa, non-Self, anātman) and the jīvātman (cetana, conscious). Neither category is fit for grief, the paṇḍitas understand both, and Arjuna who claims learning but still grieves reveals that he has not yet surrendered ahaṃtā-mamatā (I-ness and mine-ness) to Śrī Kṛṣṇa, which is the only complete resolution Puṣṭi-mārga recognizes.
doctrinal_renderings.bhashya_anchor: vairāgya-jñāna-yogaiś ca premṇā ca tapasā tathā / ekena api dṛḍhena īśaṃ bhajan siddhim avāpnuyāt — 'by even one of these held with firmness, the devotee who worships the Lord attains fulfilment'

doctrinal_renderings.rendering: Śrīdhara reads the verse as Kṛṣṇa catching Arjuna in a self-contradiction that even Kṛṣṇa's earlier rebuke (kutastvā kaśmalam — 'wherefrom has this defilement come upon you?') had already attempted to dissolve. Arjuna recites the words of the paṇḍita-class (prajñā-vādān bhāṣase) — speaking of dharma, of ancestors, of lineage — but he is not himself a paṇḍita, because a true paṇḍita does not grieve either those who have died or those who survive bereft. Śrīdhara's devotional inflection is gentle: the entire arc of grief, speech, and finally silence before Kṛṣṇa is itself a bhakta's path — Arjuna's breakdown is the prelude to complete surrender, and 2.11 marks the exact moment Bhagavān takes over the teaching.
doctrinal_renderings.bhashya_anchor: tatra kutastvā kaśmalam ityādinā mayā bodhito'pi punaś ca prajñāvatāṃ vādān ... kevalaṃ bhāṣase natu paṇḍito'si — 'though already addressed by me with
still you merely speak the words of the wise without being one yourself'

doctrinal_renderings.rendering: Madhusūdana identifies two distinct mohas (delusions) layered in Arjuna and insists both must be dissolved separately. The first is universal: the superimposition of saṃsāra-dharmas (birth, death, loss) onto the ātman, which is svaprakāśa-paramānanda-rūpa (self-luminous, of the nature of supreme bliss) and untouched by the three upādhis (gross body, subtle body, causal avidyā). The second is Arjuna-specific: the misperception that yuddha (battle), because it involves hiṃsā (violence), is adharma — a confusion Kṛṣṇa will address as the chapter proceeds. Grief (śoka) is not separately cured; it dissolves automatically when its cause — the superimposition — is removed. As Madhusūdana puts it with the rope-snake analogy: once you directly perceive the rope, the fear generated by the snake-illusion cannot persist, not even residually, because knowledge of the rope's reality is stronger than the habit of the error.
doctrinal_renderings.bhashya_anchor: ātmā svaprakāśa-paramānanda-rūpe sthūla-sūkṣma-śarīra-dvaya-tatkāraṇāvidyākhyo'pādhi-traya-avivekena ... śoka-kāraṇa-nivṛttyaiva nivṛtteḥ — 'the self is of the nature of self-luminous supreme bliss; grief ceases only through the removal of its cause [the three-upādhi confusion], not by any separate remedy'

so_what_questions.question: Kṛṣṇa says Arjuna is speaking wisely but not being wise — is that a problem I recognize in myself, where I can articulate a principle but consistently fail to live by it?
The paṇḍita is defined here as someone who does not grieve the dead or the living — what would it actually feel like not to grieve, and is that emotional flatness or something else entirely?
Arjuna's breakdown happened precisely when he had to act, not when he was sitting safely discussing philosophy — at what moment does my theoretical understanding collapse under real pressure?
The verse implies there are things worth grieving and things that are aśocya (ungrievable by nature) — how do I distinguish genuine loss from the loss of something that was never mine to keep?
Arjuna's grief is framed as a form of category error — confusing the body for the person — so what category errors might be structuring my own suffering right now?
Every school agrees the teaching starts here because Arjuna has hit a wall he cannot think his way past — what in my own life requires a teacher rather than more thinking?

The verse does not console Arjuna — it diagnoses him — and that is Kṛṣṇa's first move as a teacher: is there a difference between what I want from the people I turn to in crisis (comfort) and what would actually help me (diagnosis)?

everyday_applications.application: When a colleague leaves, a project ends, or a relationship changes, notice the grief and then ask: what exactly is it that has been lost? Advaita's answer from 2.11 is that what you identified with — a role, a relationship, a configuration — was never the ātman of either party. Practice pausing before the reactive story ('they abandoned me', 'it's over') and locating what, if anything, in the actual situation is grievable. The paṇḍita's non-grief is not suppression; it is accurate perception.

Rāmānuja's reading makes this verse actionable as a pre-decision discipline: before a difficult conversation, a hard call at work, or an obligation you are tempted to avoid, ask whether your reluctance is rooted in dehātma-confusion — the assumption that the discomfort you feel belongs to the deepest you, rather than to the body-mind apparatus. Your action in that moment is the upāya; doing it fully is itself the bhakti. Avoidance dressed as sensitivity is the same error Arjuna makes.

Madhva's emphasis on sva-manīṣā-uttha-vacanāni (words arising from private mind alone) is a daily corrective against motivated reasoning. When you find yourself constructing a sophisticated argument for inaction — one that sounds principled but happens to serve your comfort — ask: is this Śāstra speaking, or is this my own mind cosplaying as Śāstra? The paṇḍita's non-grief is not a feeling; it is the result of correctly locating agency outside the ego.

Vallabha's instruction that even one path held with dṛḍhatā (firmness) is sufficient translates practically: you do not need a complete philosophical system to begin. Pick the one thing — a daily prayer, an act of service, a practice of offering your work — and hold it without yielding when it is inconvenient. Ahaṃtā-mamatā (I-ness and mine-ness) dissolves not through argument but through the sustained practice of giving what you are most tempted to claim.

Śrīdhara's reading invites you to notice that Arjuna's collapse is not a failure but a turning point — the moment he stopped performing and started listening. In your own life, when you reach a point of genuine exhaustion with your own positions and arguments, that is not weakness; that is the bhakta arriving at the threshold. The practical move is to stop trying to solve it, and to put the question to the Bhagavān-principle — teacher, practice, or text — with the same completeness Arjuna does when he says śiṣyas te'haṃ ('I am your disciple') two verses later.

Madhusūdana's rope-snake analogy has a direct modern application: emotional residue from grief, anxiety, or shame often persists long after you have 'understood' the situation intellectually, because understanding at the propositional level has not penetrated the upādhi (conditioning layer) that generated the emotion. His point is that the remedy must go to the root — the identification with the limited self — not to the symptom. In practice this means that self-inquiry (asking 'to whom does this grief appear?') is not a bypass of feeling but the only thing that actually removes the cause.

self_assessment.bhashya_anchoring: HIGH — every rendering quotes or closely paraphrases the supplied Devanāgarī bhāṣya; no rendering relies on standard English Gītā traditions
self_assessment.doctrinal_divergence: HIGH — six genuinely distinct positions: Advaita (ātmajñāna alone, mūḍha-diagnosis), Viśiṣṭādvaita (dehātma-confusion + yuddha as upāya), Dvaita (sva-manīṣā vs Śāstra, Hari's sovereignty), Śuddhādvaita (multiple paths each sufficient, prasāda-frame), Bhakti-philological (Arjuna's breakdown as turning point, surrender as sequel), Advaita-bhakti (two-layer moha analysis, rope-snake as grief-mechanism)
self_assessment.sanskrit_constraints: CLEAN — Sanskrit terms appear in IAST with parenthetical glosses; no English suffixes on Sanskrit roots
self_assessment.verse_significance: CAPTURED — all six renderings treat 2.11 as the inaugural teaching moment, not a transitional verse; the foundational divergence (what exactly is wrong with Arjuna, and what exactly is the remedy) is the axis of distinction across schools
self_assessment.concerns: Madhva's bhāṣya in the payload is substantially shorter than the other five; the Dvaita rendering is proportionally thinner. If a full Madhva bhāṣya text (Gītā-Bhāṣya) is available for loading, the Dvaita entry should be expanded in a subsequent pass.
